name: Bud Freeman
abstract: Lawrence "Bud" Freeman (April 13, 1906 – March 15, 1991) was an American jazz musician, bandleader, and composer, known mainly for playing tenor saxophone, but also the clarinet.

works.t: Variations on a Theme by George Gershwin

quotes.text: Now, most interesting thing, I don't want to dismiss talking about Jess, but as you know, I did not see, over a period of years, I did not see too much of him and didn't have a chance to work with him. But this is the thing that happened, George Wein gave me a 50th anniversary concert, I think you were there, or I think Stanley was there, at Carnegie Hall about four years ago, and Jess came from the Coast and played on the show.
quotes.caption: Bud Freeman recalls 50th anniversary concert with Jess Stacy

quotes.text: Yes, just on the publicity he's gotten, you know, making a comeback and of course, the New Yorker article, which is a tremendous piece. That is big international stuff, I think he would get the greatest surprise of his life, because when we played in the trio, we played -- we did a duet date since we were recording a duet, we had Cliff Leeman on drums and Jess and Cliff didn't hit -- they didn't get on too well together, so we decided without offending Cliff, who is a super drummer, that we would do a session with just piano and drums -- and George Wean heard about this and so he put us on to Carnegie Hall and we played two num-
quotes.caption: Freeman recalls trio gig with Jess Stacy and Cliff Leeman

about_quotes.speaker: George Wein
about_quotes.evidence: I played with some very good musicians who edited their music: Bud Freeman on tenor saxophone and Pee Wee Russell on clarinet. I worked with them six weeks while I was in college
about_quotes.doc_title: George Wein — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: I had gotten the Austin Highschool Gang then. By that time I had Davey Tough... Bud Freeman on tenor saxophone... and we were doing this vaudeville.

about_quotes.speaker: Ralph Sutton
about_quotes.evidence: And the band was Benny Morton on trombone, Yank Lawson, Billy Butterfield, Bud Freeman, Bob Wilber, Gus Johnson on drums, Bob Haggart and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ralph Sutton interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 22, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: I’d heard of Bud Freeman and Sidney Bechet, but I hadn’t heard of Jimmy yet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, April 26, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: John Altman
about_quotes.evidence: He also was jamming with Bunny Berigan and Bobby Hackett and Bud Freeman.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Altman interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 14, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: recorded his first album as a leader with Bud Freeman in 1976
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997
